title: Born Looking Up at the Bottom
topic: Birth and Family in Calhoun County
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop in a Southwest Atlanta library branch in 2013, Lonnie C. King opens with his roots in Arlington, Georgia, in Calhoun County, his mother Bertha Lee Smith King, one of seventeen children, his father L.C. King Sr., their divorce when he was two, and his line that he was born not on the bottom but looking up at it.

title: The Evangelist Who Wrote on My Slate
summary: King credits his grandfather, the evangelist Reverend Joseph P. Smith, a note singer with perfect pitch who raised him until he was eight and taught him to read before school, invoking John Locke on the first six years writing on a child's blank slate, including the lesson that one day they would have to change the system.

title: What Is a Nigger, Granddaddy
summary: He recounts picking up a leaflet dropped by a crop duster campaigning for Eugene Talmadge in 1942, asking his grandfather what the slur meant, and the grandfather pulling him onto his knee to explain racism and to say they were still in a form of slavery, only allowed to go home at night, and that the NAACP would one day set them free.

title: The Rattlesnake in the Cornfield
summary: King describes his grandfather as a sharecropper farming ten rented acres in Southwest Georgia and Northeast Alabama, never sure he broke even, tells the anecdote of running from a rattlesnake in the cornfield and not being beaten for it, and the lesson always to tell the truth because a lie has to be remembered.

part: Atlanta and the Butler Street Y
title: Joining the Church at Eight
summary: Sent to Atlanta at eight after his grandfather's death, King recalls joining Ebenezer Baptist Church the first night of the spring revival as Martin Luther King Sr. opened the doors, founding the Ebenezer Youth Organization, sharing a paper route with A.D. King, and watching the young Martin Luther King Jr. practice preaching upstairs on Thursday nights.

part: Atlanta and the Butler Street Y
title: Look Out for My Son
summary: He describes his mother holding him back a grade and taking him at eight to the Butler Street YMCA, organized in the 1890s, and how, learning from an eighty-five-year-old man named Eugene Thomas while researching his dissertation, he found that his mother had asked director J.D. Winston to keep the boy until six o'clock when she got home from her job as a maid.

part: Atlanta and the Butler Street Y
title: We All Chipped In Fifty Cents
summary: King recalls the trust of an era when parents did not fear for their children, his mother checking secretly on his behavior, and his shock on learning from Eugene Thomas that there was no budget for the fifty cents a day he was paid as a boy: the senior staff had each chipped in fifty cents to pay the ten-year-old.

title: A Kitty Cruise
summary: Unable to afford Morehouse tuition after a partial scholarship, King describes entering the Navy on a short Kitty Cruise just before turning eighteen, the boot-camp role as educational petty officer for his company, and being assigned with the only other low-scoring sailor to the deck force to chip paint and scrub while the nineteen white men got skilled jobs.

title: Be the Best Ditch Digger Ever Born
summary: He tells of being assigned to clean the head for two hundred deck-force sailors and remembering Dr. Mays's charge to do any job better than anybody but God, so he requisitioned paint and brass polish and made the restroom so bright that a petty officer petitioned to take the easy job from him and put him back on the deck force.

title: We Don't Want Any Negroes Down Here
topic: The Fight for the Disbursing Job
summary: King recounts applying to be a disbursing clerk striker, being told by a Southern officer named Klaren that they wanted no Negroes there, and how a lieutenant from Louisiana named Horne listened to his story, took him to that same officer, and ordered him transferred, one white man telling another he was wrong.

title: The Only One Who Passed
summary: He describes the disbursing crew's cold silence until Welty Wolf of Seattle offered to teach him the work, John Bloom of Tempe joining to teach him to type, and his taking the November 1955 exam alongside four white third-class petty officers and being the only one to pass, after which the resentment broke open.

title: I'm Going Back to Atlanta
summary: King recalls telling a Navy friend, Everett Render, in 1957 that he was going back to Atlanta to finish Morehouse because the time was coming to throw off segregation and he wanted to be there, and his mother's earlier insistence to the recruiter that her son not become a see-going bus boy, which spared him steward duty but not the deck force.

title: Joe, Why Don't We Organize
summary: He describes reading of the Greensboro Four sitting down on February 1, 1960, at Yates and Milton drugstore, recruiting his friend Joseph Pierce, and persuading a reluctant Julian Bond, a former Time magazine intern who could write, to sign on so the three of them could organize and tell the story.

title: The Presidents Had Good Spies
summary: King recalls building from the Morehouse football players and Clark's James Felder at a first meeting, then being summoned within days to Harkness Hall, where Rufus E. Clement and Benjamin Mays, leaning on Roy Wilkins and the NAACP, argued the students were sent to get an education, not start a revolution they could do later.

title: I Think the Students Are Right
summary: He contrasts the presidents who echoed Clement, Manley of Spelman and Brawley of Clark who said a sit-in would embarrass him, against Harry V. Richardson of the Interdenominational Theological Center, who broke the agreed front to back the students, joined by Frank Cunningham of Morris Brown, splitting the council and leaving King to speak for the students.

title: Write a Manifesto
summary: King explains Clement's counter, that the students draft an intellectual manifesto for the public, which King read as a chess move to make philosophers of them rather than activists, and his choosing Roslyn Pope to edit it with Julian Bond and Charles A. Black, English majors who could write a great document.

title: It's Show Time
summary: He recounts Roslyn Pope calling the night before the deadline to say the committee had done nothing, his telling her it was show time, and her writing An Appeal for Human Rights and typing it at Howard Zinn's house, a document the presidents approved almost wholesale and published in the Atlanta papers, the New York Times, the Nation, and the Harvard Crimson, with Senator Jacob Javits reading it into the Congressional Record and a 1962 Miles College document essentially reprinting it with Birmingham swapped for Atlanta.

part: One Voice for the AU Center
title: A Divide and Conquer
summary: King describes a rump group under a mathematics professor at Atlanta University trying to sit in first, and his appeal to Rufus E. Clement that separate sit-ins by Clark, AU, and Morehouse would let the merchants ask whom to negotiate with, arguing the common denominator was that all were African Americans facing the same discrimination downtown.

part: One Voice for the AU Center
title: Dr. Cross, Sit Down
summary: He recounts Clement convening all AU students at Sale Hall, backing a single coalition, and a professor jumping up three times to challenge King's right to lead until Clement told him to sit down, after which the man's contract was not renewed; the schools then formed a policy council of three representatives each who chose King as founding chairman.

part: One Voice for the AU Center
title: Written by Somebody From Moscow
topic: The State Under Siege, the Volcano Erupts
summary: King reads Clement's slow-down as acceptance that events had overtaken the presidents, who feared for their tax status after Governor Ernest Vandiver claimed the appeal could only have come from Moscow and had Attorney General Eugene Cook check the HBCUs' nonprofit standing, and frames segregation as a volcano simmering below a cultural surface that the Greensboro sit-in made erupt, seventy thousand students raising sand across the South in one hundred twenty days, the postwar generation ideal shock troops whose distant parents could not be fired in reprisal.

title: Go Over to Spelman
summary: King recalls struggling to organize beyond thirty or forty people between March and May 1960 and his mother telling him to get the Spelman women behind him because the Morehouse, Clark, and Morris Brown men would follow the girls, and an invitation from Albert Manley to debate Roy Wilkins at Spelman over whether to return to class.

title: If Not Now, When
summary: He describes the packed Spelman hall, Roy Wilkins arguing smoothly for the affirmative, and his own use of a public-speaking teacher's advice to damn with faint praise: lavishing true praise on Wilkins, then pivoting to ask, if not now, when, and if not us, who, winning the crowd and leading to the May 17 march of nearly four thousand.

title: One Hundred Sixty-Nine Spelman Women
summary: King says his research identified one hundred sixty-nine Spelman women who went to jail, more than any campus, with Morehouse second, Clark third, Morris Brown fourth, and argues the Spelman women were the backbone of the movement because they took up a job to be done without contesting who led it.

title: Massive March on the Capitol
summary: King recounts announcing to the state NAACP a march on the Capitol for May 17, 1960, to commemorate the Brown decision, the Atlanta Constitution headline that sent the town wild, Governor Vandiver calling out the State Patrol with billy clubs and dogs, and Benjamin and Sadie Mays warning him through police chief Herbert Jenkins of a coming mob.
related_events: March on the Georgia State Capitol

title: Never Sacrifice a Principle for Peace
summary: King describes Mays asking him not to march for fear he would be hurt, his reply quoting Mays's own speech, never sacrifice a principle for peace, and Mays asking where he would end up, to which King said Wheat Street Baptist Church; Mays secretly followed and later wrote in Born to Rebel that he saw King had matured.

title: Walking With the Chief
summary: King tells of marching on May 17 with thousands as chief Herbert Jenkins fell in beside him warning of danger, his decision to ask Jenkins for a direct order to turn at Whitehall Street so that one student beaten by a billy club could not hand the movement to its enemies, and how about two hundred marchers who got lost reached the Capitol, where Charles A. Black led them through menacing heckling without a beating, with young African American men he later spotted in photographs whom he suspects were a black vigilante group.
related_events: March on the Georgia State Capitol

title: Doused With Acid in West End
summary: King describes launching a grocery-store boycott, targeting Mann Brothers in West End where a hostile newspaper editorial had compared him to Hitler, and a white man of about fifty-seven lunging from a crowd of three hundred hecklers to douse him with an acid-like substance that burned his skin and clothes and splashed Charles A. Black and Frank Holloway.

title: A Pool Hall Emptied
summary: He recalls being refused water at one service station and helped at the next after the attack, hearing it reached the news, and how an honest black pool hall in West End emptied of men who ran to see what was happening, leaving him convinced Atlanta came close to a Birmingham-style conflagration that would have gone hard against blacks.

title: Nonviolence as a Tactic
summary: King explains that his committee, unlike Nashville, ran no nonviolence workshops and held that nonviolence was a tactic, not a way of life, a practical and inexpensive approach, and begins to turn to the institutions of the Atlanta movement.

title: The Slow Dollar and the Fast Dime
summary: King recounts the hostile Atlanta Daily World, hiring Julian Bond to write a publication called the Student Movement and You, and the fall plan to boycott downtown with Rich's department store as the kingpin, applying economics lessons about the fast dime over the slow dollar and a Domino Theory framing, then sitting in at Rich's Magnolia Room in June 1960 with Howard Zinn's family and being taken by chief Jenkins not to jail but to meet Richard Rich, who boasted of his record with Negro customers and told King to take his students back to class, to which King promised to return in the fall with thousands.

title: A Newspaper to Fight Back
topic: M. Kasuth Hill and the Inquirer
summary: King tells of a light-skinned black businessman, M. Kasuth Hill of Hill Office Supplies, coming to his office angry that the Atlanta Daily World was selling out human dignity for advertising, and proposing they start a newspaper using his offset press and Hill's rights to the Atlanta Inquirer name, with Julian Bond and the Gibson brothers forming its core for the first issue in July 1960.

title: This Is Your Time to Get Hurt
summary: He recounts boycotting Colonial and A&P stores, C.A. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World summoning him and spitting as he warned he would lose a nine-thousand-dollar Colonial ad if picketing continued, and King answering that in every revolution some people get hurt and this was Scott's time, then steering black retailers to move their ads to the Inquirer.

title: A Letter From the Fulton County Jail
topic: Hill, the Inquirer, and the Jail Letter
summary: King spells out M. Kasuth Hill's name and lineage as son of the city's first African American doctor, credits himself and Julian Bond with writing the Inquirer's articles, and recalls writing a letter from the Fulton County jail during the October 1960 jailing when he and Martin King shared a cell as movement leaders.

title: Pennington the Cartoonist
summary: King praises Maurice Pennington as an illustrator who, but for his race, would have ranked with Herbert Block, describes a coming AU Library exhibit of Pennington's thirty-odd cartoons and over a hundred movement photographs, and an agreement with Mrs. Pennington to lend the materials for five years.

title: You Have to Get Organized
summary: King recalls recommending to his committee that he ask Martin King to convene the student leaders into a region-wide organization, the meeting with Ella Baker, King, and a few students, and his argument, witnessed by Otis Moss Jr., that the South's segregationists were organized from Maryland to Texas passing near-identical laws, so the movement had to organize to beat them.

title: A Battle Between Her and Me
summary: He describes disagreeing with Ella Baker, who wanted a grassroots movement organization and used an Indian expression for her position, while Martin King agreed with King and put up five hundred dollars for Baker to arrange the Easter-weekend conference, held at Shaw University in Raleigh, to which six Atlanta students drove in a rented white Buick provided by Dr. Asa Yancey of the Atlanta Medical Society.

title: A Junior Arm of SCLC
summary: King recalls some three hundred people forming SNCC at Shaw, doubts King intended it as a junior arm of SCLC since the charismatic leader never raised it with him, and recounts the chairmanship battle when Nashville put forward Marion Barry, whom he had never heard of, against King's own nomination, expecting it would have been Diane Nash.

title: The Citadel of Atlanta
summary: He describes the compromise making Marion Barry temporary chairman while moving SNCC's headquarters to Atlanta, where the Atlanta group paid the rent for the first months, and argues Atlanta was the logical home as the citadel of civil rights thinking since W.E.B. Du Bois, a genius the city long failed to honor.

title: They Overlooked the Lady
summary: King calls Diane Nash the Nashville movement itself, articulate, committed, and the real leader the others followed, and says he never understood, except perhaps as sexism, how James Bevel, Marion Barry, and John Lewis leapfrogged over her, naming her a true unsung hero who risked her life.

title: Names From Other Towns
summary: He runs through the leaders he had heard of beyond Atlanta, David Forbes at Shaw, Ed King expelled from Kentucky State, Marvin Robinson at Southern, Chuck McDew at Orangeburg, the students pushed out of Alabama State until Bernard Lee joined them in Atlanta, and later Hank Thomas and Reginald Robinson, while never meeting the four from Greensboro.

title: Written Out of the Story
summary: King insists on remembering Ed King, expelled from Kentucky State, the second person after Jane Stembridge to staff the SNCC office and effectively run it, articulate, courageous, and close to Marion Wilson, whom he searched for only to find had died in 2012 in Prince George's County, and distinguishes him from the white Ed King of Mississippi.

title: Wait Until October the Nineteenth
summary: King describes building the fall campaign of sit-ins and boycotts, noting Rich's later loss of ten million dollars, and battling A.D. King and Bernard Lee, who wanted to act immediately, while he insisted on waiting until October 19 to coincide with the presidential election, prevailing because the Spelman delegation under Herschelle Sullivan backed him.

title: You Can't Lead From the Back
topic: Persuading Martin King to Sit In
summary: King recounts arranging for Martin Luther King Jr. to join the October 19 sit-in at Rich's, King hesitating over an existing probation from a DeKalb County arrest while his father and Wyatt Walker were on the phone, and King winning him over by invoking the elder King's own sermon that you cannot lead from the back, you have to lead from the front, and citing their long friendship since 1945.

title: But for That Arrest
topic: The Kennedy Call and the Election
summary: King ties Martin King's arrest to a firestorm in the black community and the Kennedy intervention, the call to Coretta Scott King that switched votes in Illinois, Michigan, and beyond, and his belief that but for the arrest Nixon would have been president, noting Nixon had prepared but withheld a statement of support through E. Frederic Morrow rather than buck the boss.

title: The Walkie-Talkie Chess Game
summary: King credits a retired master sergeant he calls El Comandante with turning his ideas into a plan, and describes countering the merchants' tactic of merely closing lunch counters by deploying walkie-talkie teams downtown with a central radio post, so spotters could send students right back when a counter reopened, a system built by Ronald Yancey, the first black graduate of Georgia Tech.

part: Testing Boynton and the Freedom Rides
title: Testing Boynton v. Virginia
topic: From the Tests to the Freedom Rides
summary: King recounts that after the December 1960 Boynton v. Virginia ruling against discrimination in interstate commerce, his committee sent teams to test it in Chattanooga, Columbia, Jacksonville, and Birmingham, with Maurice Pennington and Mays Dillard arrested in Chattanooga and Charles A. Black and Otis Moss Jr. hustled out of Athens, and how James Farmer phoned about the tests, recalled his own earlier attempt outside the South in the 1940s, and said he would mount the Freedom Rides in the spring, a plan they discussed at Clark College and that a February 1961 Marvin Rich letter to Roy Wilkins in the NAACP files confirms.

title: Snookered Into a Meeting
summary: King recounts the white powers and sidelined Negro leaders meeting behind his back to settle the boycott on their terms, and Jesse Hill Jr., trusted by King, luring him to a chamber of commerce meeting billed as the most important of his life, where the merchants he had been battling presented a proposition to desegregate after the schools were integrated.

title: I Baptized You, Boy
summary: He describes Martin Luther King Sr. rising to say he was tired of King and had baptized him, and the irony that his defender was Frank H. Neely, chairman of Rich's board, who had been tapping King's leg with his cane yet declared that King alone had a constituency while the leaders had appointed themselves, after King asked to take the proposition back to his group.

title: That's a Short Time
summary: King recounts the recess where Rufus Clement told him he was between a rock and a hard place, and John C. Calhoun, a former NAACP head who had gone to jail rather than surrender its membership list, urging him at sixty, after a lifetime of waiting, to accept this first voluntary Southern desegregation as a short wait, leading King to agree on two conditions, that it be in writing and that he present it to his students.

title: A Literal Firestorm
summary: He tells of Ivan Allen Jr. holding a press conference before King could reach campus, announcing the students had agreed to end the boycott, the firestorm that followed, King and Herschelle Sullivan resigning only for the students to refuse the resignation, and a mass meeting of over two thousand at Warren Memorial Methodist Church where a nurse in white silenced Martin Luther King Sr. and the place erupted.

title: The Greatest Speech of His Career
summary: King recalls phoning Martin King to calm the booing crowd, and King delivering what King calls the greatest speech of his career, surpassing the March on Washington, taking the crowd to the mountaintop, warning that the cancerous disease of disunity would defeat them, and quieting a powerful meeting of conflict between students, young Turks, and old guard, with a quiet white observer at the back.

title: Send Me Your Credit Card
summary: King reflects that the sober realized the town had never been at such a place before, and tells how, at the height of the boycott, he asked people to mail him their Rich's credit cards and three hundred fifty-three did, kept in a safety deposit box and returned afterward, proof that the movement had reached the masses, not the classes, with the fast dime over the slow dollar.

title: The Greatest Movement Since Slavery
summary: King casts his life as a continuation of his grandfather's NAACP recruiting, calls himself one of at least a hundred who played similar roles, and frames the 1960s civil rights movement as the greatest since the end of slavery, a volcano whose eruption, like a giant firecracker reaching its apogee, would eventually settle.

title: The Embers That Reached Everyone
summary: He argues that when African Americans won Title VII with its clause against discrimination by race, color, creed, national origin, and sex, the embers spread to the women's, Latino, and gay movements, the irony being that the masses of blacks remained at the back of the bus economically while others advanced, which he attributes partly to inadequate preparation.

title: A Blanket and a Cell Phone Light
summary: King closes with a homeless young woman who studied by cell-phone light under a blanket after lights-out in a shelter and is entering Spelman as a junior on full scholarship to become an oncologist, his lesson that one must work to rise from looking up at the bottom, and his hope to convince more people to write the right things on young people's slates rather than blame the system.
